brief.topic: a kid sees water in three forms in one day — ice in the freezer, water from the tap, steam from the kettle; the question asks the kid the three states of matter water can be in

passageRow.subject: science
passageRow.title: Water in Three Forms
passageRow.body:
## Water in Three Forms

The diagram shows three pictures. The first picture is of ice cubes in a freezer. Ice is cold and hard. The second picture shows water flowing from a tap. This water is clear and wet. The third picture shows steam rising from a kettle. Steam is hot and looks like a cloud. All three pictures show water. Water can be ice, liquid, or steam. Each form looks different. But they are all water.
passageRow.topic: a kid sees water in three forms in one day — ice in the freezer, water from the tap, steam from the kettle; the question asks the kid the three states of matter water can be in
passageRow.topicNotes: Students observe water in solid, liquid, and gas forms.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What is ice?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario states that ice is cold and hard.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: Why is steam hot?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario explains steam rises from a kettle, which heats the water.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What did the kid see in picture 2?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario describes picture 2 showing water flowing from a tap.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What happens if ice is heated?
questionRows.explanation: Heating ice melts it into water, as shown in the scenario.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: Which form is like a cloud?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario describes steam as looking like a cloud.

brief.topic: two kids play tug-of-war; the stronger kid pulls harder and the rope moves their way; the question asks the kid why the rope moves toward the stronger puller

passageRow.subject: science
passageRow.title: Tug-of-War Experiment
passageRow.body:
## Tug-of-War Experiment

Two kids play tug-of-war in the yard. One kid is bigger and stronger. The other kid is smaller. They each hold one end of the rope. The bigger kid pulls harder. The rope moves toward the bigger kid. The smaller kid tries to pull back. But the rope still moves toward the bigger kid. The kids laugh and try again. They switch sides and play again.

The rope always moves toward the stronger puller. The kids see who can pull the hardest.
passageRow.topic: two kids play tug-of-war; the stronger kid pulls harder and the rope moves their way; the question asks the kid why the rope moves toward the stronger puller
passageRow.topicNotes: Demonstrates observation of cause and effect in force and motion.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What did the kids do?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario describes the kids playing tug-of-war with a rope.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: Why did the rope move?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario states the bigger kid pulled harder, so the rope moved toward them.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What happens when they switch sides?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario shows that the rope moves toward the stronger puller, regardless of sides.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What did the kids notice?
questionRows.explanation: The kids noticed that the rope always moves toward the stronger puller.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What if both kids pull equally?
questionRows.explanation: If the pull is equal, the forces balance and the rope stays still.

brief.topic: a kid measures their shadow at three times — short at noon, long in the morning and afternoon; the question asks why the shadow is shorter at noon

passageRow.subject: science
passageRow.title: Measuring Shadows
passageRow.body:
## Measuring Shadows

A kid stands outside. They measure their shadow three times. In the morning, the shadow is long. At noon, the shadow is short. In the afternoon, the shadow is long again. The kid writes down the shadow lengths. They see the shadow is shortest at noon. The sun is high in the sky at noon. This makes the shadow short.
passageRow.topic: a kid measures their shadow at three times — short at noon, long in the morning and afternoon; the question asks why the shadow is shorter at noon
passageRow.topicNotes: Observing how the sun's position affects shadow length.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What did the kid see at noon?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario states the shadow is shortest at noon.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: Why was the shadow short at noon?
choices: The sun was behind the kid
questionRows.explanation: The scenario explains the sun is high at noon, making the shadow short.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: When is the shadow longest?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario mentions long shadows in morning and afternoon.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What happens if the sun is low?
questionRows.explanation: Low sun creates longer shadows, as seen in morning and afternoon.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: Why measure shadows at different times?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario involves observing shadow changes over time.

brief.topic: a picture shows three Texas birds — hummingbird (long thin beak), hawk (sharp curved beak), duck (wide flat beak); the question asks the kid which beak is best for catching small fish

passageRow.subject: science
passageRow.title: Bird Beaks in Texas
passageRow.body:
## Bird Beaks in Texas

The diagram shows three birds. The first bird is a hummingbird. It has a long, thin beak. The second bird is a hawk. It has a sharp, curved beak. The third bird is a duck. It has a wide, flat beak.

Each bird has a different beak shape. The hummingbird's beak is good for reaching into flowers. The hawk's beak is strong for tearing food. The duck's beak is flat for scooping up small things in water.
passageRow.topic: a picture shows three Texas birds — hummingbird (long thin beak), hawk (sharp curved beak), duck (wide flat beak); the question asks the kid which beak is best for catching small fish
passageRow.topicNotes: Described diagram of bird beaks and their uses.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What beak does the hummingbird have?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario states the hummingbird has a long, thin beak.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: Why is the hawk's beak sharp?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario says the hawk's beak is strong for tearing food.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What would a duck use its beak for?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario explains the duck's beak is flat for scooping up small things in water.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What happens if a hummingbird's beak is short?
questionRows.explanation: The hummingbird's long beak helps it reach into flowers; a short beak would prevent this.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: Which bird has a flat beak?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario shows the duck has a wide, flat beak.

brief.topic: a kid holds two magnets together — one way they snap together, the other way they push apart; the question asks the kid what magnets can do besides stick to metal

passageRow.subject: science
passageRow.title: Exploring Magnet Magic
passageRow.body:
## Exploring Magnet Magic

Sam has two small magnets. He holds them close together. The magnets snap together. Sam turns one magnet around. Now, the magnets push apart. Sam tries again. One way they snap together. The other way they push apart. Sam learns that magnets can stick together. They can also push away from each other. He wonders what else magnets can do. He knows they can also stick to metal. Sam is excited to learn more about magnets.
passageRow.topic: a kid holds two magnets together — one way they snap together, the other way they push apart; the question asks the kid what magnets can do besides stick to metal
passageRow.topicNotes: Observing magnet behavior besides sticking to metal

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What did Sam see first?
questionRows.explanation: Sam saw the magnets snap together when held close.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: Why do magnets push apart?
questionRows.explanation: Magnets push apart when the same poles face each other.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What happens if Sam turns a magnet?
questionRows.explanation: Turning a magnet can make them push apart if same poles face each other.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What else can magnets do?
questionRows.explanation: Sam knows magnets can stick to metal.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What if Sam uses a bigger magnet?
questionRows.explanation: A bigger magnet might have a stronger force.

brief.topic: a kid sees a big rock by a creek that has small bits of sand and pebbles around it; over time the rock got smaller; the question asks what makes a big rock turn into small bits

passageRow.subject: science
passageRow.title: Rock and Sand by the Creek
passageRow.body:
## Rock and Sand by the Creek

The diagram shows a big rock by a creek. There are small bits of sand and pebbles around it. Water flows over the rock every day. Over time, the rock gets smaller. The small bits of sand and pebbles grow bigger. The water helps break the rock into small pieces. The rock changes slowly. This is how a big rock turns into sand and pebbles. The creek shows how nature changes things over time.
passageRow.topic: a kid sees a big rock by a creek that has small bits of sand and pebbles around it; over time the rock got smaller; the question asks what makes a big rock turn into small bits
passageRow.topicNotes: Observe and describe how erosion breaks rocks into smaller pieces.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What did the kid see by the creek?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario mentions a big rock by the creek.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: Why does the rock get smaller?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario explains that water helps break the rock into small pieces.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What happens to sand over time?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario states that the small bits of sand and pebbles grow bigger.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What if water stopped flowing?
questionRows.explanation: Without water, the rock would not break down into smaller pieces.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: How does nature change things?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario describes the rock changing slowly over time.

brief.topic: a picture series shows the four stages of a butterfly: egg, caterpillar, chrysalis, butterfly; the question asks the kid which stage comes after the caterpillar

passageRow.subject: science
passageRow.title: Butterfly Life Stages
passageRow.body:
## Butterfly Life Stages

The diagram shows the life of a butterfly. First, we see a small egg on a leaf. Next, the egg hatches. Out comes a caterpillar. The caterpillar eats and grows. After that, the caterpillar forms a chrysalis. Inside the chrysalis, it changes. Finally, a butterfly comes out. The butterfly can fly. It lays eggs, and the cycle starts again.

Look at the stages. Egg, caterpillar, chrysalis, butterfly. What comes after the caterpillar?
passageRow.topic: a picture series shows the four stages of a butterfly: egg, caterpillar, chrysalis, butterfly; the question asks the kid which stage comes after the caterpillar
passageRow.topicNotes: Observe the sequence of butterfly stages.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What is the first stage?
questionRows.explanation: The diagram shows the life starting with an egg on a leaf.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What happens after the caterpillar?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario states the caterpillar forms a chrysalis next.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: Why does the butterfly lay eggs?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario explains the butterfly lays eggs to start the cycle again.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What if the caterpillar does not eat?
questionRows.explanation: Caterpillars need to eat to grow; otherwise, they stay small.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What does the butterfly do last?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario shows the butterfly lays eggs last to start the cycle.

brief.topic: a kid pours rice and dried beans together in a bowl and stirs; then they pick out the beans; the question asks the kid whether the rice and beans changed when mixed

passageRow.subject: science
passageRow.title: Mixing Rice and Beans
passageRow.body:
## Mixing Rice and Beans

Tom has a bowl. He pours 2 cups of rice into it. Then, he adds 1 cup of dried beans. Tom stirs them together with a spoon. The rice and beans are mixed up. Tom picks out the beans one by one. He puts them in another bowl.

Did the rice and beans change? Tom looks at them. The rice is still rice. The beans are still beans. Mixing did not change them. The rice and beans are the same as before.
passageRow.topic: a kid pours rice and dried beans together in a bowl and stirs; then they pick out the beans; the question asks the kid whether the rice and beans changed when mixed
passageRow.topicNotes: Matter and energy: mixing substances does not change their properties

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What did Tom pour first?
questionRows.explanation: Tom poured rice first, as stated in the scenario.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: Why did Tom stir the bowl?
questionRows.explanation: Tom stirred to mix the rice and beans, as described.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What happened to the rice and beans?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario states that the rice and beans were mixed.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What if Tom added water?
questionRows.explanation: Adding water could start cooking them, a common kitchen process.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: Did the rice change?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario notes the rice remained unchanged.

brief.topic: a class fish in a tank needs three things — water, food, and a clean tank; the question asks the kid what would happen if the fish ran out of food

passageRow.subject: science
passageRow.title: Fish Needs in a Tank
passageRow.body:
## Fish Needs in a Tank

The diagram shows a fish tank. Inside the tank, there is one fish. The fish needs three things. First, it needs water to swim in. Second, it needs food to eat. Third, it needs a clean tank to stay healthy.

The tank has a filter to keep the water clean. There is a container of fish food next to the tank. What happens if the fish runs out of food? The fish might get hungry and weak. It is important to give the fish food every day.
passageRow.topic: a class fish in a tank needs three things — water, food, and a clean tank; the question asks the kid what would happen if the fish ran out of food
passageRow.topicNotes: SE: Observe needs of organisms in environments

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What does the fish need to swim?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario states the fish needs water to swim.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: Why is the filter important?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario explains the filter keeps the water clean.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What happens if the fish has no food?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario says the fish might get hungry and weak without food.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What if the tank is not clean?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario mentions a clean tank is needed for health.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What must you do every day?
questionRows.explanation: The scenario states it is important to feed the fish daily.

brief.topic: a kid leaves two cups of water — one in the sunny window, one in the shade — for an hour; the cup in the sun feels warmer; the question asks why the sunny cup is warmer

passageRow.subject: science
passageRow.title: Sunny and Shady Water
passageRow.body:
## Sunny and Shady Water

A kid puts two cups of water on a table. One cup is in the sun. The other cup is in the shade. The kid waits for one hour. Then, the kid touches both cups. The cup in the sun feels warm. The cup in the shade feels cool.

The kid wonders why the sunny cup is warmer. The sun shines on the cup in the window. The shade keeps the other cup cooler.
passageRow.topic: a kid leaves two cups of water — one in the sunny window, one in the shade — for an hour; the cup in the sun feels warmer; the question asks why the sunny cup is warmer
passageRow.topicNotes: Observing temperature changes due to sunlight exposure

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: Why is the sunny cup warm?
questionRows.explanation: The sun shines on the cup, making it warm.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What did the kid feel?
questionRows.explanation: The kid felt the sunny cup was warm and the shady cup was cool.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What happens if both cups are in sun?
questionRows.explanation: If both cups are in the sun, both become warm.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: What keeps the shady cup cool?
questionRows.explanation: The shade keeps the cup cool by blocking the sun.

questionRows.subject: science
questionRows.question: Why does the sun warm the cup?
questionRows.explanation: Sunlight heats objects it shines on, like the cup.
